meta.title: Frontier Tower — Open Metaverse Hackathon
meta.description: Spatial memory of the first Open Metaverse Hackathon, March 7-8 2026. Knowledge capsules from omb.wiki, Resonance Lab, and the ROOM hyperblog — spatialized.

nodes.label: Frontier Tower — Lobby & Entrance
nodes.description: The street-level threshold at 995 Market St. Where the spatial internet begins.

nodes.label: 16th Floor — Hackathon Space
nodes.description: Where builders gather. The primary action space of the Open Metaverse Hackathon.

nodes.label: The Alien Lounge
nodes.description: Silver mylar walls, glow-in-the-dark stars, the Arecibo message. A room that takes its own mythology seriously.

nodes.label: Arrival at Frontier Tower
nodes.description: March 7th, 2026. Walking into 995 Market St for the first Open Metaverse Hackathon.

nodes.label: The Build — 48 Hours
nodes.description: Building ROOM at the intersection of Tools, Places, and AI tracks. Proving that places can hold memory.

nodes.label: Demo Day
nodes.description: Sunday afternoon. The spatial internet has memory now.

nodes.label: Discovering the Lounge
nodes.description: A break from building. Down the elevator, through a door, into another world entirely.

nodes.label: The Collective Hippocampus
nodes.description: If the hippocampus lets an individual navigate remembered space, ROOM builds that architecture at collective scale.
nodes.content:
Your brain has dedicated architecture for spatial memory. Place cells fire when you're *here*. Grid cells create abstract coordinate systems. ROOM takes this biological principle seriously.

By anchoring knowledge to spatial coordinates, we're not just making data spatially accessible — we're making it neurologically compatible with how humans actually remember.

ROOM reduces spatial memory to four atomic types:
- **Place** — where it happened
- **Event** — what happened
- **Perspective** — what it meant
- **Artifact** — what it produced

Navigation through ROOM becomes epistemic archaeology: walking through space is walking through time, through perspective, through sedimentary layers of collective understanding.

nodes.label: The Spatial Fabric
nodes.description: RP1's three-tier map of the metaverse: Celestial → Terrestrial → Physical. DNS for space.
nodes.content:
The Metaverse Spatial Fabric (MSF) is the map of the metaverse. Unlike bounded game engines (~10km), RP1's map spans hundreds of millions of light years.

**Three Tiers:**
1. **Celestial** — Galaxies, star systems, planets
2. **Terrestrial** — Root → Land → Country → State → City → Parcel
3. **Physical** — GLB objects, Gaussian splats, avatars

The browser doesn't download the map. It queries a Map Service for objects near the user's position — like DNS for space.

nodes.label: Places Can Remember
nodes.description: The web gave us pages. The spatial internet will give us places — where knowledge inhabits rather than sits on surfaces.
nodes.content:
The web gave us pages — flat surfaces covered in text, linked by addresses. The spatial internet will give us places — volumetric environments where knowledge doesn't sit on surfaces, it inhabits them.

ROOM explores what happens when a place doesn't just look like somewhere — when it remembers what happened there, holds the perspectives of everyone who passed through, and offers that memory to anyone willing to walk in.

This is the core thesis: spatial memory as infrastructure.

nodes.label: Open Standards — No Walled Gardens
nodes.description: The metaverse will either be built like the web — by many, for many — or locked into proprietary systems.
nodes.content:
Five non-negotiables from the Open Metaverse:
1. **Interoperability** — No software lock-in
2. **No Device Lock-In** — Phones, AR glasses, VR, desktops
3. **Ownership** — You own your data, content, identity
4. **Frictionless Access** — Browse to enter. No app downloads.
5. **Open Tooling** — Creator ecosystem, not a closed platform

AOL built a walled garden. Netscape and open HTTP won. The same pattern applies to spatial computing.

nodes.label: The Missing Semantic Layer
nodes.description: Most spatial tooling solves rendering, not meaning. The semantic layer is almost entirely missing from the open stack.
nodes.content:
Spatial information organizes into four ascending layers. Most tooling today operates on layers 2–4: rendering, networking, interaction. The semantic layer — what makes geometry into meaning — is almost entirely absent.

Without semantics, every layer above remains mute. Geometry without semantics is topography without territory. A photorealistic render has no way to declare what it depicts, who was there, what it meant.

ROOM provides the primitives that let the other layers speak. That's the gap this project fills.

nodes.label: Knowledge Sovereignty
nodes.description: Your thinking remains yours. You choose what surfaces, and how.
nodes.content:
Shared memory systems have historically demanded a sacrifice: your private cognition becomes the property of the platform. ROOM refuses this bargain.

**Three visibility levels:**
- `private` — Local only. Encrypted at rest. No network exposure.
- `shared` — Visible to invited participants. Permission-gated.
- `public` — Open. Verifiable. Indexed by the spatial internet.

Your thinking remains yours. The graph is federated — no central authority controls what gets remembered or how perspectives get weighted.

nodes.label: Digital Villages
nodes.description: Communities forming around shared spatial memory. Not platforms — villages.
nodes.content:
The Alien Lounge isn't just a weird room. It's a prototype of something important: a space that accumulates lore. Myths, inside jokes, ritual significance — the things that turn a location into a place.

Digital villages form the same way physical ones do: through repeated visits, shared stories, accumulated meaning. The spatial internet needs infrastructure for this — not just rendering, but remembering.

ROOM's ontology system lets different communities bring different schemas. The architectural view of this room is just walls and mylar. The mythological view is an alien transmission station. Both are valid. Both coexist.

nodes.label: Shards of Memory
nodes.description: Knowledge doesn't arrive whole. It arrives as fragments — shards that find each other over time.
nodes.content:
A perspective contributed to ROOM isn't a finished thought. It's a shard — an incomplete piece of understanding that gains context from proximity to other shards.

This is how actual memory works. You don't remember events as complete narratives. You remember fragments: a sound, a face, a feeling about the light. Your hippocampus weaves them together when you revisit the place.

ROOM's refinement pipeline mirrors this: capture raw → structure → refine → cross-reference → discover connections you didn't know existed. Knowledge becomes emergent rather than authored.

nodes.label: Resonance Lab — Field Notes
nodes.description: Three builders at the edge of spatial computing, personal AI, and collective knowledge. A field note from the hackathon floor.
nodes.content:
Resonance Lab is a loose collaboration between three builders working at the edges of spatial computing, personal AI, and collective knowledge. Not a finished plan — a shared sense that something important was converging.

What the hackathon clarified: spatial environments are proximity-based service delivery systems. RP1 isn't building a virtual world; it's building a browser. Services register into a shared coordinate system and activate when you're near them.

Bret explores AI carrying personal identity over time. Joshua builds infrastructure for collective memory. Mike asks whether alignment between people can be made visible before they've found each other.

Most details are still unresolved. The most interesting discoveries are probably the ones that surprise all of us.

nodes.label: ROOM Hyperblog
nodes.description: The living document explaining ROOM's thesis, architecture, and vision.

nodes.label: Resonance Lab — Episode 02
nodes.description: Field notes from the hackathon floor. Where Bonfires meets ROOM.

nodes.label: world.json — The Data Format
nodes.description: A flat, portable JSON graph — human-readable, no build step, compatible with any spatial browser.

tour.title: Frontier Tower — Walking Through Spatial Memory

waypoints.narration: You're standing in the lobby of Frontier Tower, 995 Market St. March 7th, 2026. The first Open Metaverse Hackathon starts upstairs. This building is about to become a place that remembers.

The threshold. Past this point, every conversation, every insight, every late-night breakthrough gets captured — not as surveillance, but as spatial memory. The building becomes a collective hippocampus.
16th floor. The hackathon space. Desks, screens, the hum of builders. This is where ROOM came alive — proving that the semantic layer is the missing piece of the open metaverse stack.
The spatial fabric: RP1's three-tier map system. Celestial, Terrestrial, Physical. Every building in the skyline will eventually have a Parcel. DNS for physical space.

Place cells. Grid cells. Your brain has dedicated architecture for spatial memory. ROOM mirrors it: four atomic types — Place, Event, Perspective, Artifact. Walking through space is walking through time.
Most spatial tooling solves rendering. ROOM solves meaning. Geometry without semantics is topography without territory. The semantic layer lets every other layer speak.
The web gave us pages. The spatial internet gives us places. Knowledge doesn't sit on surfaces here — it inhabits them. This is what we built.
One more thing. Your thinking stays yours. Private, shared, or public — you choose. No platform owns what you remember. The graph is federated.
Down the elevator. Through a door. Silver mylar, glow-in-the-dark stars, the Arecibo message on the wall. The Alien Lounge. A place that takes its own mythology seriously.
This room is a prototype. Digital villages form the way physical ones do — through repeated visits, shared stories, accumulated meaning. Different communities, different ontologies, same space.
Knowledge arrives as fragments. Shards of memory that find each other over time. ROOM's pipeline mirrors this: capture, structure, refine, cross-reference, discover.
Sunday afternoon. Demo day. The spatial internet has memory now. Places can remember. And you just walked through the proof.
